words.shortDefinition: The practice of merging all developers' working copies to a shared mainline several times a day.
words.longDefinition: Abbreviated as CI. It involves automatically building and testing the code every time a change is committed, allowing teams to detect problems early and improve software quality.
words.techContext: Our CI pipeline runs the full suite of unit tests on every pull request to ensure no regressions are introduced.
words.exampleSentence: By adopting continuous integration, we significantly reduced the amount of time we spent resolving merge conflicts at the end of the week.

words.shortDefinition: The practice of automatically releasing every code change that passes the automated tests to production.
words.longDefinition: Abbreviated as CD. It goes a step further than Continuous Delivery (which makes code ready for release) by actually performing the release to customers without manual intervention.
words.techContext: With continuous deployment, our new features are live for users within 10 minutes of the code being merged into the main branch.
words.exampleSentence: We moved from manual releases to continuous deployment to speed up our feedback loop with customers.

words.shortDefinition: A set of automated processes that allow developers to reliably and efficiently compile, test, and deploy their code.
words.longDefinition: A logical grouping of jobs that are executed in a specific order (stages). A typical pipeline includes stages for linting, unit testing, building a Docker image, and finally deploying to an environment.
words.techContext: The CI pipeline failed during the 'build' stage because of a missing dependency in the package.json file.
words.exampleSentence: I spent the morning optimizing our deployment pipeline to run the tests in parallel, cutting our build time in half.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a series of pipes that transport fluid (data/code) from one end to another.

codeExample.code:
stages:
  - lint
  - test
  - deploy

lint_job:
  stage: lint
  script: npm run lint

words.shortDefinition: The output files produced by a build process.
words.longDefinition: These are the compiled binaries, JAR files, Docker images, or documentation generated during the 'build' stage of a pipeline. Artifacts are often stored and then used in subsequent 'deploy' stages.
words.techContext: The pipeline uploads the compiled .apk file as an artifact so the QA team can download and test the latest build on their devices.
words.exampleSentence: We configured the CI server to keep build artifacts for 30 days so we can easily roll back to a previous version if needed.
words.etymology: from Latin arte ('by skill') + factum ('thing made').

words.shortDefinition: A deployment strategy that uses two identical production environments to minimize downtime.
words.longDefinition: One environment (Blue) handles the live traffic, while the new version is deployed to the other (Green). Once the Green environment is verified, the load balancer is switched to point to Green. Blue is kept as a backup for instant rollback.
words.techContext: We use blue/green deployments to ensure that we never have to take the site offline while we're updating the backend services.
words.exampleSentence: The switch from blue to green was successful, but we had to roll back to blue when we noticed an increase in database errors.
words.etymology: Color-based metaphor for two distinct, identical environments.

words.shortDefinition: A deployment strategy where a new version is rolled out to a small subset of users first.
words.longDefinition: The new version is tested with a fraction of real traffic before being rolled out to the entire user base. If the 'canary' group experiences no issues, the release continues. This limits the blast radius of potential bugs.
words.techContext: We released the new search algorithm to 5% of our users as a canary release to monitor its impact on server latency.
words.exampleSentence: The canary release caught a memory leak that our automated tests missed, saving us from a total production outage.
words.etymology: Metaphor for 'canaries in a coal mine' used to detect toxic gases before they affected the miners.

words.shortDefinition: Automated messages sent from apps when something happens.
words.longDefinition: A way for one application to provide other applications with real-time information. A webhook delivers data to other applications as it happens, meaning you get data immediately. It is an HTTP POST request triggered by an event.
words.techContext: We set up a GitHub webhook to trigger our CI pipeline every time a developer pushes code to the repository.
words.exampleSentence: The Slack notification was sent via a webhook triggered by the successful completion of the deployment job.

words.shortDefinition: The software or machine that executes the jobs in a CI/CD pipeline.
words.longDefinition: The runner picks up pending jobs from the CI server, executes the defined scripts (usually in an isolated environment like a Docker container), and reports the results back. They can be hosted by a provider or self-hosted.
words.techContext: We had to add more build runners because our PR queue was getting too long and developers were waiting an hour for their tests to run.
words.exampleSentence: The build runner failed to start because it couldn't connect to the Docker socket on the host machine.
words.etymology: from 'build' + 'runner' (one who carries out a task).

words.shortDefinition: A logical step in a CI/CD pipeline.
words.longDefinition: Pipelines are divided into stages (e.g., Build, Test, Deploy) to organize jobs. All jobs in a single stage usually run in parallel, and the next stage only begins if all jobs in the current stage succeed.
words.techContext: The pipeline stopped because a job in the 'Test' stage failed, preventing the code from moving to the 'Deploy' stage.
words.exampleSentence: We added a 'Security' stage to our pipeline to run an automated vulnerability scan on our dependencies.
words.etymology: from Latin stagium ('a place for standing').

words.shortDefinition: The automated checking of source code for programmatic and stylistic errors.
words.longDefinition: A 'linter' is a tool that analyzes source code to flag programming errors, bugs, stylistic errors, and suspicious constructs. It is often the very first job in a CI pipeline to ensure code quality before expensive tests run.
words.techContext: The CI job failed because the linter found three unused variables and a missing semicolon.
words.exampleSentence: Adding a linting step to our CI pipeline helped us maintain a consistent coding style across the entire team.
words.etymology: from 'lint' (tiny bits of fiber removed from clothes).

words.shortDefinition: A set of practices to manage infrastructure and application configurations using Git.
words.longDefinition: In GitOps, the Git repository is the 'source of truth' for the desired state of the system. Automated tools (like ArgoCD) ensure that the actual state of the production environment matches the state defined in Git.
words.techContext: With GitOps, we deploy to production just by merging a YAML file change into the main branch of our infrastructure repo.
words.exampleSentence: GitOps gives us a full audit trail of every infrastructure change, since everything is recorded in Git commits.

words.shortDefinition: Managing and provisioning infrastructure through code instead of through manual processes.
words.longDefinition: Abbreviated as IaC. It allows teams to use tools like Terraform, CloudFormation, or Ansible to define their servers, networks, and databases in configuration files that can be versioned and automated.
words.techContext: We use IaC to ensure that our staging and production environments are identical, eliminating 'configuration drift'.
words.exampleSentence: By using Infrastructure as Code, I was able to spin up a completely new database cluster in minutes using a single command.

words.shortDefinition: A deployment strategy that replaces instances of the old version with the new version one by one.
words.longDefinition: Ensures that some instances of the application are always running during the update, providing zero downtime. It is the default deployment strategy for Kubernetes.
words.techContext: The rolling update replaced one pod at a time, ensuring that the total capacity of our service never dropped below 80%.
words.exampleSentence: We used a rolling update to roll out the new version of our API, monitoring the error rates as each new pod came online.

words.shortDefinition: Sensitive information used by the CI/CD pipeline, such as API keys or passwords.
words.longDefinition: These are stored securely in the CI provider (e.g., GitHub Actions Secrets) and are injected into the pipeline at runtime as environment variables. They are never logged or stored in plain text in the repository.
words.techContext: I added the AWS_ACCESS_KEY to our GitHub Secrets so the pipeline can authenticate with our cloud provider and deploy the app.
words.exampleSentence: Never hardcode your production database password in your pipeline config; always use CI secrets.

words.shortDefinition: Rerunning functional and non-functional tests to ensure that previously developed software still performs after a change.
words.longDefinition: In CI/CD, this refers to the automated suite of tests that run to ensure that new code hasn't 'broken' existing features. It's about preventing a 'regression' to an earlier, buggier state.
words.techContext: Our regression testing suite caught a bug in the checkout flow that was caused by a small change in the tax calculation library.
words.exampleSentence: Automated regression testing is what gives us the confidence to deploy to production multiple times a day.

words.shortDefinition: A human-readable data-serialization language commonly used for configuration files.
words.longDefinition: Stands for 'YAML Ain't Markup Language'. It is the industry standard for defining CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI) and Kubernetes resources because of its readability and support for complex nested structures.
words.techContext: I updated the .github/workflows/deploy.yml file to add a new step for running end-to-end tests.
words.exampleSentence: The pipeline failed because of a syntax error in the YAML file—I had used tabs instead of spaces for indentation.
words.etymology: Recursive acronym: YAML Ain't Markup Language.

words.shortDefinition: A workflow where changes are only merged if they pass all automated checks.
words.longDefinition: Instead of developers merging code directly, they submit a request. The CI server builds and tests the changes in isolation. Only if the tests pass is the code actually merged into the main branch.
words.techContext: We implemented a gated check-in policy so that the 'main' branch is always in a buildable and deployable state.
words.exampleSentence: My code was rejected by the gated check-in because I had forgotten to update the unit tests to match the new API.

words.shortDefinition: Running multiple jobs or tests at the same time in a pipeline.
words.longDefinition: Allows a pipeline to finish faster by utilizing multiple runners or containers simultaneously. For example, running frontend and backend tests at the same time instead of one after the other.
words.techContext: By enabling parallel execution for our test suite, we reduced our total CI time from 20 minutes down to just 5.
words.exampleSentence: We split our end-to-end tests into four parallel jobs to significantly speed up our feedback loop.
words.etymology: from Greek parallēlos ('beside each other') + execution.

words.shortDefinition: A build runner that is managed and maintained on your own infrastructure.
words.longDefinition: Unlike GitHub-hosted runners, self-hosted runners give you more control over the hardware, operating system, and software installed. They are often used for better performance or to access resources inside a private network.
words.techContext: We used a self-hosted runner to allow our CI pipeline to deploy code to our internal servers that are behind a firewall.
words.exampleSentence: The self-hosted runner is much faster because it has access to a dedicated NVMe drive for caching dependencies.

words.shortDefinition: The process of evaluating an application without actually executing the code.
words.longDefinition: Tools like SonarQube or Snyk scan the source code to find security vulnerabilities, code smells, and technical debt. It is 'static' because the code isn't running while it's being analyzed.
words.techContext: The static analysis tool flagged a potential SQL injection vulnerability in our legacy database module.
words.exampleSentence: Our pipeline includes a static analysis stage to ensure we don't merge any code that doesn't meet our security standards.
words.etymology: from Greek statikos ('causing to stand') + analysis.

words.shortDefinition: The automated coordination of multiple deployment tasks across different environments.
words.longDefinition: Manages the complex sequence of events needed to release a multi-service application, such as updating databases, clearing caches, and rolling out new container versions in a specific order.
words.techContext: We use AWS CodePipeline for deployment orchestration to manage the flow of code from GitHub to our production EKS cluster.
words.exampleSentence: Deployment orchestration ensures that our database migrations are always applied before the new application code starts serving traffic.

words.shortDefinition: A mechanism to store and reuse intermediate build results to speed up future builds.
words.longDefinition: CI systems can cache things like downloaded 'node_modules' or compiled object files. On the next run, the pipeline can download the cache instead of fetching everything from the internet again.
words.techContext: I configured the CI build cache for our Maven project, which reduced the 'Install Dependencies' stage from 4 minutes to 20 seconds.
words.exampleSentence: The build was slow today because I changed a core library, which invalidated the entire build cache.

words.shortDefinition: The practice of keeping your code in a state where it can be released to production at any time.
words.longDefinition: Unlike Continuous Deployment (which releases automatically), Continuous Delivery means every change is *ready* to go, but the final push to production may still require a manual 'approval' button click.
words.techContext: Our team uses continuous delivery, so we have a 'Deploy' button in GitLab that the product manager clicks after final verification.
words.exampleSentence: Continuous delivery ensures that our software is always in a releasable state, even if we only release once a week.

words.shortDefinition: A series of steps that run on the same build runner.
words.longDefinition: The basic unit of work in a pipeline. A job consists of multiple steps (like checking out code, running a script). All steps in a job are executed sequentially on a single runner.
words.techContext: I split the unit tests and integration tests into two separate jobs so they can run in parallel on different runners.
words.exampleSentence: The 'deploy-to-prod' job is configured to only run after the 'test' job has successfully completed.

words.shortDefinition: The maximum impact that a failure in a specific component can have on the rest of the system.
words.longDefinition: A key concept in deployment safety. By using canary releases or microservices, teams try to minimize the 'blast radius' so a bug in one area doesn't crash the entire platform.
words.techContext: We moved our payment gateway into a separate service to limit the blast radius of any potential deployment failures.
words.exampleSentence: The blast radius of the bug was small because we caught it during the canary release before it reached most of our users.
words.etymology: Metaphor from military/explosives: the area affected by an explosion.
